questions.stem: A homeowner decided to upgrade his lawn equipment and posted his five-year-old gasoline riding mower for sale on a neighborhood classifieds website. A buyer purchased the mower for three hundred dollars and took it home. The first time the buyer used it, a corroded fuel line, which had deteriorated during the homeowner's ownership, sprayed gasoline onto the hot engine and ignited, burning the buyer's leg. The buyer sued the homeowner in strict products liability, arguing the mower was in a defective condition unreasonably dangerous when sold. The homeowner had never sold a mower before, works as a schoolteacher, and sold the machine only because he was replacing it. Is the buyer's strict products liability claim likely to succeed?
choices: Yes, because anyone who sells a defective product is strictly liable for the harm it causes.
Yes, because the defect existed at the time the homeowner sold the mower.
No, because used goods can never support a products liability claim under any theory.
No, because the homeowner is a casual seller rather than a commercial supplier routinely engaged in selling goods of that type.
questions.explanation: Strict products liability requires that the defendant be a commercial supplier, meaning a person or entity engaged in the business of selling goods of the type at issue. Casual or one-time sellers and service providers are not commercial suppliers and cannot be held strictly liable, even if the product was defective when sold. A schoolteacher making a single classified-ad sale of his own used mower is the paradigm casual seller, so the strict liability claim fails, although the buyer might explore an ordinary negligence theory if the homeowner knew of and concealed the danger. Choice A omits the commercial supplier element entirely. Choice B addresses timing of the defect, which cannot cure the missing supplier element. Choice C is overbroad because negligence and other theories can still apply to used goods.

questions.subject: torts
questions.rule: Defamation — slander per se (impugning business integrity or skill)
questions.stem: At a crowded chamber of commerce luncheon, a businessman stood up and announced to the table that a local accountant, a private individual, had been systematically skimming money from her clients' trust accounts for years. The statement was false; the businessman confused the accountant with someone else and made no effort to check. Several of the accountant's clients were present and heard the remark. The accountant sued the businessman for defamation. At trial she proved the statement was false, that it was about her, that third parties heard it, and that the businessman was at least negligent, but she offered no evidence of any specific lost clients, lost fees, or other economic loss. The businessman moved for judgment, arguing that a slander plaintiff must prove special damages. Should the motion be granted?
choices: No, because a false spoken accusation that impugns a person's business integrity is slander per se, for which special damages need not be proven.
No, because special damages are never required in any defamation action.
Yes, because all slander claims require proof of specific economic loss.
Yes, because the accountant failed to prove the businessman acted with actual malice.
questions.explanation: Defamation requires a false defamatory statement of and concerning the plaintiff, publication to a third party, and damages. Slander, an oral defamatory statement, ordinarily requires proof of special damages, but not when the statement falls within slander per se, which includes statements impugning business integrity or skill, imputing a loathsome disease, unchastity, or a crime of moral turpitude. Accusing an accountant of skimming client funds attacks her professional integrity, so damages are presumed. Choice B is wrong because ordinary slander outside the per se categories does require special damages. Choice C ignores the per se exception. Choice D is wrong because actual malice is required for public officials and public figures; a private-figure plaintiff need only show fault amounting to at least negligence, which she proved.

questions.subject: torts
questions.rule: Invasion of privacy — intrusion upon seclusion
questions.stem: A landlord, curious about his tenant's personal life, concealed a small video camera inside a smoke detector in the tenant's bedroom and watched the live feed on his phone for several weeks. He never recorded the footage, never showed it to anyone, and never mentioned what he saw to a single person. The tenant discovered the camera while replacing the smoke detector's battery and was horrified to learn how long it had been transmitting. She sued the landlord for invasion of privacy. The landlord argues that he cannot be liable for any privacy tort because he never published, disclosed, or disseminated anything he observed, and because the tenant suffered no reputational or economic harm. Which of the following best states the result?
choices: The landlord is not liable, because every invasion of privacy tort requires some form of publication or public disclosure.
The landlord is not liable, because a property owner retains the right to monitor activity anywhere within his own building.

The landlord is liable for intrusion upon seclusion, because he intruded into the tenant's private affairs in a place where she had a reasonable expectation of privacy, in a manner highly objectionable to a reasonable person.
The landlord is liable for public disclosure of private facts, because the camera transmitted private information.
questions.explanation: Intrusion upon seclusion occurs when the defendant intrudes into the private affairs of the plaintiff, the plaintiff has a reasonable expectation of privacy, and the intrusion is highly objectionable to a reasonable person; classic examples include reading private mail and illegal surveillance or wiretapping. Hidden bedroom video surveillance is a textbook intrusion, and the tort is complete upon the prying itself, with no publication element. Choice A is wrong because publication is required for the disclosure and false light branches of privacy, not for intrusion. Choice B fails because a landlord's ownership does not extinguish a tenant's reasonable expectation of privacy in her leased bedroom. Choice D names the wrong branch, since public disclosure requires widespread dissemination of private information to others, which never happened here.

questions.subject: torts
questions.rule: Private nuisance — substantial and unreasonable interference with use and enjoyment
questions.stem: A rendering plant began operating around the clock on a parcel adjacent to a long-established residential street. Several times a week, the plant emitted intense odors of decaying material that drifted over the neighboring homes, forcing one homeowner to keep her windows sealed in summer, abandon her backyard garden, and cancel outdoor gatherings. Nothing physical from the plant, no particles, liquids, or objects, ever crossed onto her land in any detectable way, and her house suffered no structural damage. Air-quality readings showed the emissions complied with the odor thresholds in the plant's state operating permit. The homeowner sued the plant for private nuisance. The plant argues it cannot be liable because nothing tangible entered her property and it complied with its permit. Is the homeowner likely to prevail?
choices: No, because liability for interference with land requires a physical invasion of the property.
Yes, because the recurring odors are a substantial and unreasonable interference with her use and enjoyment of her property that would be offensive to a reasonable person.
No, because compliance with the state operating permit is an absolute bar to any nuisance claim.
Yes, but only if she proves the plant intended to drive her from her home.
questions.explanation: A private nuisance is a substantial and unreasonable interference with a person's use or enjoyment of her property, and the interference must be offensive, inconvenient, or annoying to a reasonable person. Recurring stenches that force a homeowner to seal her windows and abandon her yard easily qualify, and unlike trespass to land, nuisance requires no physical entry onto the property. Choice A confuses nuisance with trespass, which does require a physical invasion. Choice C overstates the defense: statutory or permit compliance is a factor courts may weigh, but it is not an automatic bar to nuisance liability. Choice D is wrong because nuisance does not require a purpose to harm or expel the plaintiff; an unreasonable interference suffices regardless of motive.

questions.subject: torts
questions.rule: Intentional misrepresentation (fraud)
questions.stem: A man selling his vintage sports car told a prospective buyer that the engine had been completely rebuilt the previous year by a certified specialty mechanic and had logged fewer than one thousand miles since the rebuild. The seller knew this was false; the engine had never been rebuilt, and he invented the story to justify his asking price. The buyer, an enthusiast who specifically wanted a rebuilt engine for an upcoming rally season, asked to see the mechanic's invoice, and the seller produced a convincing forged receipt. Relying on these representations, the buyer paid a premium price. Two months later the original, worn engine failed, costing the buyer thousands of dollars. The buyer sued the seller for intentional misrepresentation. Which element, if any, will be the most difficult for the buyer to establish, and what is the likely outcome?
choices: Reliance will be impossible to show because a buyer of a used car must always commission an independent inspection.
Scienter will defeat the claim because the seller's statements were merely optimistic sales talk.
The claim fails because misrepresentation requires a fiduciary relationship between the parties.
No element poses serious difficulty; the buyer can establish a knowingly false statement of material fact made to induce the purchase, justifiable reliance, and resulting damages.
questions.explanation: Intentional misrepresentation, or fraud, requires a misrepresentation of a material fact by the defendant, the defendant's knowledge that the statement was false, intent to induce the plaintiff, actual and justifiable reliance by the plaintiff, and damages. Every element is comfortably satisfied: the rebuild claim was a specific factual assertion the seller knew was false, bolstered by a forged invoice to induce the sale, and the buyer paid a premium and suffered out-of-pocket loss when the engine failed. Choice A is wrong because reliance was reasonable, especially after the buyer sought and received documentary confirmation. Choice B fails because a concrete, fabricated claim about a rebuild is a statement of fact, not puffery, and the seller's actual knowledge of falsity is scienter. Choice C is wrong because a fiduciary relationship matters to nondisclosure duties, not to affirmative lies.
